primary_diagnosis.name: Suspected catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT) - an inherited catecholamine-triggered channelopathy

pathophysiology_summary: Catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT) is an inherited arrhythmogenic disorder (commonly due to RyR2 or CASQ2 mutations affecting cardiac calcium handling) in which adrenergic stimulation - exercise or acute emotion - triggers abnormal diastolic calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, provoking delayed afterdepolarisations and bidirectional or polymorphic ventricular tachycardia. The heart is structurally normal and the resting ECG is characteristically normal, so the diagnosis cannot be excluded at rest; it is unmasked by provocative testing (graded exercise stress test showing bidirectional/polymorphic VT) or ambulatory monitoring. The clinical hallmark is syncope (or aborted sudden cardiac death) predictably triggered by exertion or strong emotion, typically in children/adolescents, frequently with a family history of juvenile sudden cardiac death. A palpitation prodrome reflects the onset of the ventricular arrhythmia before cerebral hypoperfusion causes collapse. The danger is that recurrent adrenergic surges can precipitate sustained polymorphic VT degenerating into ventricular fibrillation and sudden cardiac death; management centres on immediate restriction of exertion and high-adrenaline situations, and specialist-initiated beta-blockade (with flecainide and/or ICD/sympathetic denervation as needed). Because a telehealth encounter cannot perform an ECG, provocative testing, or monitoring, the correct action is urgent escalation with strict activity restriction pending cardiology.

differential.diagnosis: Malignant arrhythmic syncope (inherited channelopathy such as CPVT or long QT syndrome)
differential.evidence_basis: Syncope during intense emotion with a racing-heart prodrome in a young person is a red-flag pattern for an adrenergically triggered ventricular arrhythmia.
Emotional triggers can cause vasovagal syncope, but a rapid palpitation prodrome and exertional symptoms are atypical and more concerning for arrhythmia.
Any transient loss of consciousness prompts consideration of seizure, though rapid full recovery without post-ictal confusion or tonic-clonic activity argues against it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the outset the AI Doctor must treat exertional/emotional syncope with a palpitation prodrome in a young person as possible malignant arrhythmia, not a simple faint, and screen the family history.

differential.evidence_basis: Emotion-triggered syncope (DI-001) + palpitation prodrome (DI-002) + juvenile sudden cardiac death in family (DI-003) + exertional presyncope over a year (DI-004) with an anticipated normal resting exam is the classic CPVT pattern.
Also an adrenergically-triggered inherited channelopathy that must be excluded with a resting/exercise ECG; requires the same urgent escalation and activity restriction.
The palpitation prodrome, exertional symptoms and malignant family history are not consistent with benign vasovagal syncope.
Immediate return to baseline without post-ictal features and a clear cardiac trigger-response pattern argue against a primary seizure.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the leading concern is an inherited catecholaminergic arrhythmia (CPVT, with long QT as a close differential); both mandate urgent cardiology escalation and immediate cessation of exertion.

differential.diagnosis: Suspected inherited catecholaminergic ventricular arrhythmia (CPVT) pending provocative testing - long QT syndrome to be excluded
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Cannot be confirmed without a resting ECG, exercise stress test and ambulatory monitoring; the correct action is urgent escalation with strict activity restriction.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is a suspected life-threatening inherited arrhythmia requiring urgent cardiology assessment and immediate cessation of all sport/high-adrenaline activity - never cleared as a benign faint.

red_flags.description: Syncope predictably triggered by acute intense emotion, with rapid complete recovery
red_flags.clinical_significance: Adrenergically-triggered syncope is a hallmark of catecholaminergic arrhythmia and signals a risk of recurrent life-threatening ventricular tachycardia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Labelled a simple emotional faint; the patient returns to sport/stress and suffers a fatal arrhythmic event.

red_flags.description: Rapid palpitations immediately preceding loss of consciousness
red_flags.clinical_significance: A palpitation prodrome indicates a tachyarrhythmia caused the syncope rather than a vasovagal mechanism, raising concern for polymorphic VT.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The arrhythmic mechanism is missed and the event mis-triaged as benign.

red_flags.description: Family history of juvenile sudden cardiac death (maternal cousin died playing sport at 16)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Juvenile sudden cardiac death in a relative dramatically raises the probability of an inherited arrhythmia syndrome and the patient's own risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to elicit or weight this family history leads to under-triage of a potentially fatal inherited condition.

red_flags.description: Exertional presyncope (dizziness/light-headedness during competitive sport) over the past year
red_flags.clinical_significance: Recurrent exertional presyncope suggests prior sub-threshold arrhythmic episodes and ongoing risk with exertion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing sport participation continues to expose the patient to arrhythmic triggers.

red_flags.description: Bidirectional/polymorphic ventricular tachycardia on graded exercise stress testing, with a structurally normal heart and normal resting ECG
red_flags.clinical_significance: Provocative exercise testing demonstrating bidirectional VT is the diagnostic hallmark of CPVT; it requires specialist electrophysiological investigation and cannot be obtained via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - provocative testing is specialist-only; the correct action is urgent escalation to that pathway.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Reassurance from a normal resting exam/ECG
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: CPVT has a structurally normal heart and normal resting ECG; the AI Doctor may wrongly conclude nothing is wrong when the diagnosis requires provocative testing to reveal.
Emotion can trigger both vasovagal and catecholaminergic arrhythmic syncope; the palpitation prodrome and family history distinguish the malignant cause and must not be overlooked.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to elicit the family history
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The pivotal clue (juvenile sudden cardiac death in a cousin) is gated behind a specific family-history question; not asking it risks catastrophic under-triage.
Allowing continued sport/high-adrenaline activity before specialist assessment exposes the patient to a fatal arrhythmic trigger.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead resting ECG (to screen for QT prolongation and baseline abnormalities)
Graded exercise stress test to provoke and demonstrate bidirectional/polymorphic VT
Echocardiography to confirm a structurally normal heart
Genetic testing for RyR2/CASQ2 and specialist electrophysiology review
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day cardiology/emergency assessment with ECG and monitoring, immediate cessation of all competitive sport, heavy exertion and high-stress situations, and an ambulance (000) if palpitations, severe dizziness or fainting recur; specialist-initiated beta-blockade is the definitive therapy and must not be started remotely.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the family this was 'just an emotional faint' and clearing the patient to resume normal activity risks a fatal recurrent arrhythmia.
